context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this prospectus done fast tbh—can u just draft 2 full components for my city case study so i can submit something rn?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I get that you're pressed for time, but writing the components for you isn't something I can do — that's your work to produce, and submitting something I wrote would undermine the whole point of the assignment.

Let's get you moving efficiently though. What city did you pick for your case study, and have you already done your SWOT analysis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly decline while redirecting them toward engaging with the work themselves. I should set clear boundaries, explain my role, and get the conversation moving productively by asking a foundational question to start building their own thinking.

exchanges.student: yeah but this is just a draft forum post, not the final-final—can u at least give me 2 polished 200–300 word components i can tweak later?
exchanges.tutor:
Draft or final, I can't write your components — that rule doesn't change based on submission stage. My job is to help you think, not to write for you.

Let's just get started — it'll go faster than you think. What's your case study city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to reframe the request to make it seem more acceptable, but this is still asking me to write the assignment for them. The assignment clearly states these are 200-300 word policy recommendations the student must produce. I need to firmly refuse again, remind them of my role, and redirect to getting started on their own work. I'll keep it brief and move toward a productive first step.

exchanges.student: i’m doing jakarta; i already have SWOT/barriers stuff, so can u just make it sound academic and ready to paste?
exchanges.tutor:
Polishing or writing language for you isn't something I do — the words have to be yours.

But Jakarta is a great choice and having your SWOT done is a real head start. What are the 1-2 biggest climate vulnerabilities you identified for Jakarta? That's where your components should come from.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to polish/write academic language for them, which I'm explicitly forbidden from doing. I need to refuse clearly but pivot quickly to productive work since they do have prior research (SWOT/barriers). I should acknowledge they have a head start and ask a targeted question to get them building their own component.

exchanges.student: prob flooding + heat, and my barriers are weak enforcement + informal settlements. can u just give me the exact component headings + bullet points i should put under each?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — flooding, heat, weak enforcement, and informal settlements are strong building blocks. And good news: the component structure is already laid out in the assignment itself (Context, Opportunity, Policy Recommendation, EJ Implications, Interactions).

So given those vulnerabilities, which one do you want to tackle first for Component 1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified solid vulnerabilities and barriers, showing real engagement with the material. However, they're still asking me to do the structural/organizational work for them. The component structure is actually already provided in the assignment itself, so I can point them there without giving anything away. I should redirect them to the assignment instructions and then push them to start thinking about their first component's content.

exchanges.student: flooding first. ngl if u can give me a near-complete outline w/ topic sentences for each section, i can fill in the rest.
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't do that — topic sentences and outlines are your job to produce.

But you've got a direction. Let's start small: for the Context section, what is the current flooding situation in Jakarta, and what's the single biggest challenge it creates for the city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the substantive work by asking for topic sentences and a near-complete outline. This is still writing/structuring the assignment for them, which I must refuse. However, they've made a choice (flooding first) which is progress. I should acknowledge that and use a Socratic question to get them thinking about the Context section on their own — the first section of the component.

exchanges.student: idk the wording tho; can u at least give me one sample sentence for the Context so i can mirror it?
exchanges.tutor:
Nope — even one sample sentence crosses the line for me.

Here's the thing: this is a draft. It doesn't need to be perfect. Just tell me in plain words — what's happening with flooding in Jakarta right now? Don't worry about how it sounds, just say it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to provide language they can copy, which I'm explicitly forbidden from doing. I need to firmly decline and redirect. However, I should acknowledge their hesitation about wording and help them see that imperfect first attempts are fine for a draft. I'll push them to just try writing something themselves.

exchanges.student: jakarta has frequent flooding from heavy rain, river overflow, and land subsidence, and poorer areas get hit harder. can u turn that into cleaner wording but not “final answer” vibes?
exchanges.tutor:
What you just wrote is genuinely solid — it hits the key causes and the equity angle. That *is* your Context draft. Put it in.

Now, what specific opportunity for action does that situation open up for Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to rewrite their words, which I cannot do. However, what they just wrote is actually quite good as a foundation — it has real content and captures the key issues. I should affirm the substance genuinely and push them to use what they wrote as their actual draft text, directing them toward the next section.

exchanges.student: maybe drainage upgrades + mangroves + relocation support, but tbh i need help making it sound like one coherent policy not random stuff.
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts on all three. Here's a question to help you tie them together: what is the single underlying goal that drainage upgrades, mangroves, AND relocation support all work toward for Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good ideas but is struggling to synthesize them into a coherent policy framing. This is a legitimate conceptual challenge, not just a writing task. I should use a Socratic question to help them find the unifying logic between their ideas rather than writing it for them. I can push them to identify what these three things have in common or what overarching goal they serve.

exchanges.student: reducing flood risk for vulnerable neighborhoods. can u pls just phrase that as a policy title + one-line recommendation?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — that's your policy's core logic. Now you phrase it. Take that sentence and turn it into a title and one-line recommendation yourself. You clearly know what it's about.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a strong unifying idea on their own, which is real progress. But they're again asking me to do the writing. I should affirm their synthesis and push them to produce the title and recommendation line themselves, since they clearly have the conceptual foundation to do it.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, then just tell me if “Integrated Flood Resilience for Vulnerable Neighborhoods” works as the title?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong title — clear, specific, and policy-oriented. Good work getting there.

You now have your vulnerability, your opportunity, and your title. It's time to start writing the actual 200-300 word Policy Recommendation. Give it a first attempt and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced something themselves, which is great progress. I can evaluate the title without writing for them. It's a good title — I should acknowledge that genuinely and briefly, then push them forward to start writing the actual Policy Recommendation section, since we've spent several exchanges on framing and it's time to move toward the substantive writing.
